title: SSE reconnection logic creates a new EventSource but never updates eventSourceRef when reconnect succeeds, and the stale closure in setTimeout reuses the closed handle
description: In useSSE.ts lines 46-54, the onerror handler closes the EventSource and after 5s creates a new one, but `eventSourceRef.current` is only assigned the new EventSource in the 'eventSourceRef.current = new EventSource(...)' line 51, which is OK. However, the bigger bug: `eventSource.onmessage` and `onerror` are only set on the FIRST EventSource — the replacement EventSource on line 51 has no message/error handlers attached, so after reconnect the SSE connection is silent. Additionally the `setTimeout` callback captures `eventSource` (the closed one) — the guard at line 50 (`eventSourceRef.current === eventSource`) ensures it doesn't proceed, but the actual reconnect on a fresh error never wires up handlers.
suggested_fix: Factor `connect()` to a function inside useEffect that creates the EventSource, attaches `onmessage`/`onerror`, and assigns to `eventSourceRef.current`. The reconnect setTimeout should call `connect()`, not duplicate the new EventSource(...) inline.

title: useQuery() creates a new dataSignal on every call but never wires it back to entry updates — subscribed components never see refreshed data
description: In query.js line 232-234, `_dataSignal` is created on the entry only on the first call. But `fetchQuery` → `_executeEntry` writes to `entry.data` and `entry.status` directly (lines 117-119), NEVER to `entry._dataSignal`. So useQuery's `effect` that reads `dataSig.value` (line 238) only ever re-runs with the value captured at construction time, not when subsequent fetches complete. Initial fetch on line 247 manually pushes to dataSig via the `.then` callback — but on `refetch()` (line 244, which calls `fetchQuery(..., { force: true })`) the result goes to `entry.data` and `dataSig` is never updated, so UI goes stale on refetch.
suggested_fix: In `_executeEntry`'s resolve handler (after line 122), also update `entry._dataSignal.value = { data, status: 'success', error: null }` (and in the catch handler at line 132). Remove the redundant `.then`/`.catch` wrapper at lines 248-252 that duplicates this work for the first fetch only.

title: invalidate() declares opts.refetch parameter but never actually refetches — silent dead code
description: The `invalidate` method signature is `invalidate(match, opts = {})` with `opts.refetch` (line 175) and a module-level `invalidationListeners` array (line 41) that is referenced in the typedef. But the implementation (lines 176-187) only sets `entry.dataUpdatedAt = 0` — it never triggers a refetch and never pushes to `invalidationListeners`. Any code that calls `queryClient.invalidate(key, { refetch: true })` to refresh stale data will appear to succeed but produce no network activity. Combined with HS-2, stale-while-revalidate is broken.
suggested_fix: Either implement the refetch by re-running `fetchQuery(key, fetcher, { force: true })` for each matching entry that has a registered fetcher (you'd need to store the fetcher on the entry), or remove the `refetch` option and the `invalidationListeners` constant and update JSDoc to reflect 'mark stale only'.

title: _executeEntry rejects all observers on AbortError but new callers will not be re-attached, causing dropped promises
description: In `_executeEntry` lines 125-131, when the previous fetch is aborted, every waiting observer's promise is rejected with the AbortError. This is wrong: the new fetch kicked off right after (line 106-107) does NOT re-attach these observers — they were already cleared in line 129. So any caller waiting on a dedup'd fetch that gets replaced gets an AbortError reject, even though logically they should just continue waiting on the new fetch. The docstring on line 127 says 'observers wait on new promise' but the code does not implement that.
suggested_fix: Before calling `abortCtrl.abort()` on line 106, move the current `entry.observers` array to a local variable, then after creating the new abortCtrl/promise, re-attach the prior observers to the new entry's `observers` (or wrap their resolvers in a 'wait for next success' chain). The new fetch's `.then` should resolve both old and new observers.

title: App singletons (currentUser, theme, sidebarCollapsed) call subscribe() at module top-level, leaking listeners and double-firing in HMR/tests
description: Lines 178-183 register `theme.subscribe(...)` and `sidebarCollapsed.subscribe(...)` immediately when the module is imported. There is no matching unsubscribe handle exposed. Under Vite HMR (per frontend/router.js UI-4 finding, this project has the same HMR pattern) every module reload adds another localStorage write subscriber. In tests, every `import './store.js'` in a new test file adds more subscribers to the same singleton signal — they fire for the lifetime of the process and grow unboundedly. Worse, `currentUser = signal(null)` is exported but no `setCurrentUser` action — any module that writes `currentUser.value = ...` bypasses any future 'session refresh' logic.
suggested_fix: Move the localStorage subscribe() calls into an exported `initStore()` function called once from app bootstrap (shell.js), and return the unsubscribe handles so HMR can swap them. Add a `setCurrentUser(user)` action in the same file so all writes go through one funnel. Add a `__resetForTests()` helper to clear all subscribers and reset to defaults.

title: effect() cleanup is not awaited — async effects leak state across re-runs
description: The `effect()` factory at line 96-137 accepts a `fn` that may return a cleanup function (line 122: `if (typeof ret === 'function') cleanup = ret`). But if `fn` is async, the returned promise resolves to a function — the check `typeof ret === 'function'` is false for a Promise, so cleanup is silently dropped. Additionally, on every re-run, line 120 invokes `cleanup()` synchronously even if the previous `fn` is still running — so a long-running async effect can be torn down mid-flight, leaving tracked signals still subscribed to its run function. Comment on line 93 documents the cleanup pattern but only handles sync.
suggested_fix: Document that `fn` MUST be sync, or detect Promise return: `if (ret && typeof ret.then === 'function') ret.then(r => { if (typeof r === 'function') cleanup = r })`. Also, on re-run, if previous `fn` is still pending, await it before invoking cleanup. At minimum, throw a console.warn when an async function is passed.

title: All forum mutations call raw fetch() instead of api-client.js — DRY violation, missing CSRF, no shared error envelope handling
description: Lines 8, 18, 28, 71, 86, 96, 139, 155, 165, 238, 256, 276 all use the raw `fetch()` API with hand-rolled `if (!res.ok) throw new Error('...')` and a static error string. The codebase has a centralized `frontend/api-client.js` (line 16) that auto-prefixes /api/, sets CSRF, surfaces server `error.code`/`error.message`, and offers `unwrap()`. By bypassing it: (a) CSRF token is not sent for state-changing requests, (b) error messages lack server `error.message` detail, (c) no consistent 401 handling — the api-client redirects to /login on 401 but raw fetch does not, leaving mutations to fail silently with a generic Error. The comment on api-client.js line 13 confirms the project intends all client HTTP to go through it.
suggested_fix: Replace all raw `fetch()` calls with `apiPost`, `apiPatch`, `apiDelete` + `unwrap()`. Example: `const r = await apiPost(`/api/projects/${data.projectId}/forums`, data); if (!r.ok) throw new Error(...)` becomes `return unwrap(apiPost(...))`. The same fix applies to useDocumentMutations.ts, useMeetingMutations.ts, useNewsMutations.ts, useWikiMutations.ts, useNotificationMutations.ts, useTimeEntryMutations.ts, useMemberMutations.ts, useModuleMutations.ts.

title: useUnpinThread is a copy-paste of usePinThread — same URL, same method, no body, so the backend cannot distinguish pin from unpin
description: Lines 233-249 (`usePinThread`) and 251-267 (`useUnpinThread`) are byte-identical except for the success log message and the comment. Both POST to `/api/projects/{projectId}/forums/{forumId}/threads/{threadId}/pin` with no body, no `?action=unpin` query param, no header. The server's pinned handler will toggle to the same value both times — unpin never actually unpins. Compare to useLockThread/useUnlockThread (lines 207-229) which correctly pass `{ isLocked: true/false }` in the body to `updateThread`. The two functions are visually identical, hiding a real bug.
suggested_fix: Decide on a contract: either (a) follow the lock/unlock pattern and pass `{ isPinned: true }` / `{ isPinned: false }` in the body via updateThread's PATCH endpoint, or (b) append `?action=unpin` to the URL. Then update both functions to differ in that one input. Add a unit test that asserts the request body/URL for each.

title: useCreateThread onSuccess invalidates queryKeys.forum(variables.forumId) — this hits the thread LIST key, not the parent forum's metadata key
description: Line 106-107: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.forumThreads(variables.forumId) })` (correct — the thread list under that forum) and then `queryKeys.forum(variables.forumId)`. Looking at the pattern: `queryKeys.forum(id)` is the SINGLE-forum detail key, not the forum-list key. Invalidating it would refetch a single forum's detail (rare to need after creating a thread). The intended invalidation is probably the project's forum list, e.g. `queryKeys.forums(variables.projectId)`. This is a stale closure / wrong-key bug — thread creation appears to succeed but the forums list re-render is missed.
suggested_fix: Trace the queryKeys factory in queries/queryKeys.ts to confirm. If `forum(id)` is the single-forum detail (per the `forumThread(id)`/`forumPost(id)` naming pattern in line 50, 119, 214), replace line 107 with `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.forums(variables.projectId) })` to refresh the project's forum list (which displays thread counts).

title: useUpdateDocument onSuccess invalidates with `undefined` as folderId, dropping all folder-scoped caches
description: Line 81: `queryKeys.documents(variables.projectId, undefined)`. The intended invalidation is probably `variables.folderId` (or the documents list scoped to whichever folder the document lives in). The `useCreateDocument` invalidation at line 65 uses `variables.folderId` correctly. Passing `undefined` to the factory generates a different key (or no folder filter), so existing folder caches are NOT invalidated — list views stay stale after a rename.
suggested_fix: Read variables from the response (`onSuccess: (data, _vars)`) — `data.folderId` is the source of truth after the server confirms. Use `queryKeys.documents(variables.projectId, data.folderId)`. Or, in the absence of response shape, just use the broader `['documents']` prefix match as a fallback, which is already done on line 83 (so the duplicate invalidation is harmless but the specific one is wrong).

title: Zero onError handlers across 11 mutation hooks — user-facing errors silently disappear
description: useCreateForum (line 35), useUpdateForum (line 46), useDeleteForum (line 59), useCreateThread (line 103), useUpdateThread (line 115), useDeleteThread (line 127), useCreatePost (line 172), useUpdatePost (line 184), useDeletePost (line 196), useLockThread (line 210), useUnlockThread (line 222), usePinThread (line 236), useUnpinThread (line 254), useVotePost (line 274) — none define an `onError` callback. When a mutation fails, the caller receives `mutation.error` but no toast is shown, no logging is sent to Sentry, and no user feedback. The store.js layer (line 194 `pushToast`) has a working toast API that nothing here wires up. Same gap exists in useDocumentMutations.ts, useMeetingMutations.ts, useWikiMutations.ts, useNewsMutations.ts, useNotificationMutations.ts, useTimeEntryMutations.ts, useMemberMutations.ts, useModuleMutations.ts.
suggested_fix: Add a shared `onErrorToast(queryClient, err, msg)` helper in `lib/mutationError.ts` (or similar) and wire it into every `useMutation` call. The helper should: (1) push a toast via `useUIStore`/`pushToast` with `err.message`, (2) `console.error` the full error, (3) optionally capture in Sentry. A single point of change reduces 30+ missing handlers to one.

title: useCreateDocument invalidates documents() with the new document's folderId — but the document may have been moved from another folder, leaving the old folder's cache stale
description: Lines 64-69: `useCreateDocument` invalidates `documents(projectId, variables.folderId)` and `documentFolders(projectId, variables.folderId)`. The input `variables.folderId` is the *new* document's folder. If the API supported moving a document between folders (or if `folderId` is initially null and the server assigns one), the original folder's list would be stale. More pressingly, if `variables.folderId` is `undefined`/null (e.g. root-level document), the invalidation key is different from the actually-rendered list key — root-level docs may be cached under `documents(projectId, 'root')` or `documents(projectId)`. This is a class of issues, not just this one site — see also useCreateFolder line 142 (uses `parentId` correctly but doesn't refresh the children of the parent folder's parent).
suggested_fix: In `onSuccess`, prefer to read the response's actual server-assigned `folderId`/`parentId` and invalidate against that. When the response shape is unknown, do a prefix-match invalidation: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['documents', projectId] })` to refresh all folder-scoped lists for that project.

title: documents() key factory takes only projectId, but callers pass (projectId, folderId) — silent key collision, invalidations never match
description: Line 82 defines `documents: (projectId: string) => ['documents', projectId] as const` — single-arg. But useDocumentMutations.ts calls `queryKeys.documents(variables.projectId, variables.folderId)` (line 65, 81) and `queryKeys.documents(variables.projectId, undefined)` (line 81) — extra arg is silently ignored. So the key is ALWAYS `['documents', projectId]`, regardless of folderId. All folder-scoped list caches share one key, causing: (a) per-folder data leakage (one folder's docs visible in another), (b) wrong invalidation (invalidating one folder overwrites another). Similar concern: `useCreateDocument` line 65 calls `queryKeys.documents(projectId, variables.folderId)` and the factory ignores folderId, so list views for ALL folders get refetched (loud but correct) — but the per-folder data in cache is not actually per-folder.
suggested_fix: Change line 82 to: `documents: (projectId: string, folderId?: string | null) => ['documents', projectId, folderId ?? null] as const`. Update useDocuments.ts (if it exists) to use the same factory. Audit all callsites for the new signature. This restores the per-folder cache separation the callers assume.

title: useWorkPackages `enabled` expression is a no-op tautology — does not actually gate the query
description: Line 147: `enabled: !filters?.projectId || !!filters.projectId`. This simplifies to `enabled: true` (either filters is undefined and !undefined===true, or filters is defined and !filter?.projectId is false OR !!filters.projectId is true, so it's always true). The JSDoc on line 146-147 says 'Enable when projectId is present, or when no filter needed' — but the OR makes both clauses redundant. Compare to useMembers (line 12) and useWorkPackage (line 158) which correctly use `enabled: !!projectId`. Here the fetch fires unconditionally, hitting `/api/work-packages` with no projectId for callers that forgot to pass one (potentially returning global data, slow query, RBAC issue).
suggested_fix: Replace with `enabled: !filters || !!filters.projectId` (i.e. allow either no filter OR one with a projectId). Add a unit test that asserts the fetch is NOT made when an unfiltered hook is mounted without a projectId param, and is made when projectId is set.

title: useDeleteRelation onSuccess invalidates ['work-packages'] (the whole prefix) instead of just the two affected work packages' relation caches
description: Line 278-281: comment says 'we don't know which WP pair was affected', so it falls back to invalidating the entire `['work-packages']` prefix. But the mutationFn on line 277 receives the relation `id`, and the API has to know the from/to work package to delete the row. Either: (a) include fromId/toId in the mutation variables and invalidate both (like useCreateRelation does at lines 262-267), or (b) the server response should include them and use it in onSuccess. As written, every relation delete refetches the entire work-packages tree, which is a 4-5× over-fetch for a single relation row delete. Also there is NO optimistic update or rollback.
suggested_fix: Change the mutation to accept `{ id, fromId, toId }`. In onSuccess, invalidate `queryKeys.workPackageRelations(fromId)` and `queryKeys.workPackageRelations(toId)`. Add `useUpdateRelation` (currently missing) for editing relation type/direction. Consider optimistic remove via `setQueryData` with rollback in onError.

title: useQuery enabled uses workPackageId, but queryKey uses `workPackageId ?? ''` — empty-string key still gets cached
description: Line 38-42: when `workPackageId` is undefined, `queryKey` resolves to `['work-packages', '', 'watch']` and `enabled: false` prevents the fetch. But the entry is still CREATED in the cache at that key (line 38-39, the useQuery call always allocates). On the next mount with a real id, the cache returns the prior empty-string query entry and refetches — but if the id is the same as a previously-failed empty-string watch, the cache may serve stale 'no watch data' for a real work package. This is a known TanStack Query footgun. A subtler bug: in `useToggle` (line 62-68) the mutation is called without checking if `workPackageId` exists — the `!` non-null assertion at line 45 will throw on undefined.
suggested_fix: Guard `toggle` with `if (!workPackageId) return`. Use `queryKey: queryKeys.workPackageWatchers(workPackageId ?? '__disabled__')` to avoid empty-string cache keys, or skip the query entirely (early return null) when undefined. Consider adding a useEffect cleanup that removes the empty-string key on unmount.

title: useUpdateWorkPackage optimistic update spreads data over old, but data fields like statusId are objects — naive spread drops nested object refs
description: Lines 200-210: `queryClient.setQueryData<WorkPackage>(queryKeys.workPackage(id), (old) => old ? ({ ...old, ...data }) : old)`. The `data` object contains fields like `statusId` (string), but if it ever contains nested objects (e.g. `assignee: { id, name }`), the spread replaces the entire assignee object — losing `assigneeId` consistency. More importantly, the optimistic update is APPLIED to the SINGLE work-package key, but the LIST key `['work-packages', filters]` (line 144) is never optimistically updated, so any list view (table, board) shows stale data until onSettled refetches. On rollback (line 215-219) only the single key is restored; the list stays stale until onSettled.
suggested_fix: In onMutate, also walk all `['work-packages', filters]` list caches and patch the matching item in each. On rollback, restore both single and list snapshots. Document that `data` must be flat key-value patches (no nested objects) or implement a deep merge. Consider a normalized cache (Entity Adapter) to avoid this manual syncing.

title: useSearch queryKey includes types/limit/offset as object references in array — non-serialized keys cause cache misses on rerender
description: Line 75: `queryKey: ['search', queryParams.q, queryParams.projectId, queryParams.types, queryParams.limit, queryParams.offset]`. `queryParams.types` is an array `['work_package', 'wiki']` — TanStack Query hashes queryKeys with JSON.stringify, so this is fine for caching. BUT, `queryParams.limit` and `offset` are not part of `queryParams` (they ARE in the destructure `params` but the spread `queryParams = { ...params, q: debouncedQuery }` is on line 72 — actually they ARE included). The real bug: queryKeys should be defined in a `queryKeys.search(projectId, types, page, limit)` factory like every other domain, to be consistent and allow targeted invalidation. Currently a search invalidation requires `[ 'search' ]` prefix which works but is ad-hoc.
suggested_fix: Add to queries/queryKeys.ts: `search: (projectId: string | undefined, types: string[], q: string, page: number) => ['search', projectId ?? null, types, q, page] as const`. Use it in useSearch. Add `useClearSearch` mutation that invalidates `['search']` on success.

title: Sequence handler hardcodes `parts.length !== 2` — silently ignores longer sequences like 'g i t' (git-style) or 'a b c'
description: Line 108: `if (parts.length !== 2) continue` rejects any sequence with more than two keys. The file header at line 8 says sequences like 'g d' are two keys, but does not restrict the format. The implementation will silently drop 'g i t' or any longer chord. The `continue` on line 108 means the shortcut is registered but never fires — users get no feedback. Also: when the FIRST key of a multi-key sequence is pressed, the function returns on line 114 (after setting seqBuffer) — but if the user is in the middle of a different shortcut, that other shortcut's first key is also captured. Sequence tracking has no concept of multi-pending sequences.
suggested_fix: Implement N-key sequence support: `seqBuffer` should be an array of recent keypresses within the timeout, and the matcher walks the buffer looking for any registered sequence that is a suffix. Alternatively, document in the file header that only 2-key sequences are supported, and add a runtime check that warns `console.warn('Multi-key sequence ${cfg.combo} not supported')` when parts.length > 2.

title: useEffect has empty deps array — shortcuts array is captured at mount and never refreshed
description: Line 147: `}, []).` The effect uses `normRef.current` (line 104), which is set in a SEPARATE useEffect on lines 86-94 with `[shortcuts]` deps. But the handler closure on line 100 captures the initial `normRef` (ref objects are stable, so that's OK) — it reads the CURRENT value of `normRef.current` at each keypress, so updates to `shortcuts` will be reflected. Good. However, the second useEffect on line 99-147 has empty deps, so on every render the `handler` is RE-ATTACHED (`addEventListener` + `removeEventListener`) because of React StrictMode double-invocation. Combined with HMR this can pile up listeners (each removal is correct, but in StrictMode dev the add-remove-add sequence can race). More importantly, `seqBuffer = useRef(null)` (line 97) is a module-level-style ref that persists across re-renders but the timeout check on line 140 reads `Date.now() - seqBuffer.current.at` — if the buffer is from a different shortcut set, the timing is wrong.
suggested_fix: Move `seqBuffer` ref inside the effect, or reset it on every effect run. Document that `shortcuts` is captured at mount (intentional or not — if intentional, rename to `initialShortcuts` or freeze the reference). For the StrictMode/HMR concern, add an integration test that mounts/unmounts the hook 100 times in StrictMode and asserts only one keydown listener is active.

title: useActivity useQuery refetchInterval 30s fires forever — no cleanup if projectId changes to undefined
description: Line 45: `refetchInterval: 30000` (30s polling) on a query that is `enabled: !!projectId` (line 44). When the projectId prop changes from a real value to undefined, the query disables and stops fetching — but the refetchInterval timer is internal to TanStack Query and should be cleaned up. Verified behavior: TanStack does clean up its refetch timer on disable. So this is OK. REAL bug: when `projectId` changes from one project to another (e.g. user navigates from project A to project B), the refetchInterval keeps firing for the OLD query key (`['activity', 'A', filters]`) and a new one starts for B. The old cache entry stays 'fresh' for 30s but the data is for the wrong project. There is no `useEffect` to clear the previous cache or reset the interval.
suggested_fix: Use `queryKeys.activity(projectId, filters)` from the central factory. Add `useEffect` cleanup: when projectId changes, call `queryClient.removeQueries({ queryKey: ['activity', oldProjectId] })`. Or: in the page component, wrap the hook in a key={projectId} to force remount. Better: switch to SSE (like useSSE.ts) for realtime, drop the polling.

title: dismissedIds is read from localStorage on every render — not reactive, no useSyncExternalStore
description: Line 42: `const dismissedIds = typeof window !== 'undefined' ? getDismissedIds() : []`. Called inside the hook body on every render. The `dismiss` function (line 49-51) writes to localStorage but does NOT update React state — so the announcement list does not re-filter after dismiss. The user clicks 'dismiss', the row stays visible, the next page load shows it gone. Also: the `useQuery` does not know about localStorage, so the cache returns the same data; the filter is just an in-memory `data?.filter(...)` that needs a re-render to recompute. Since dismiss doesn't trigger a re-render (no setState), the row stays.
suggested_fix: Track dismissed IDs in a `useState<Set<string>>` initialized from localStorage, and update state inside `dismiss`. Or: use a Zustand store (matching the ui-store pattern) for dismissed announcements so other components can react. Re-render after dismiss.

title: useModuleMutations invalidates ['project', projectId] (singular) but useProjects and use-permission use ['projects', projectId] (plural) — invalidation never matches
description: Line 16: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['project', projectId] })`. Every other hook in the codebase uses the plural form `['projects', projectId, 'membership']` (use-permission.ts line 46) or `['projects', projectId, 'members']` (useMembers.ts line 6) or `['projects']` (use-projects.ts line 20, 39, 54, 64). The invalidation here generates a key `['project', projectId]` — does not match any existing query — so toggling modules on a project shows no visible cache refresh, and the UI continues to show the stale enabled/disabled state until manual page refresh. The mutation also has no onError handler.
suggested_fix: Change line 16 to `['projects', projectId]` (plural) to match the project-scoped key namespace. Add an `onError` that pushes a toast (see HS-11 helper). Consider invalidating `['projects', projectId, 'modules']` if such a query key is registered.

title: usePermission uses ad-hoc queryKey ['projects', projectId, 'membership'] not in central queryKeys factory — DRY violation
description: Line 46: hard-coded `['projects', projectId, 'membership']`. Every other domain in the codebase (work packages, forums, meetings, documents, wiki, news, time, notifications) routes its keys through `queries/queryKeys.ts` (124 lines, 30+ factories). Membership is the odd one out. If a future rename/reshape happens (e.g. add `?include=role` query param to the membership key), there's no central place to update. Also the test on line 60 — `if (!data?.role?.permissions) return false` — silently returns false if the role.permissions field is missing, which is a security-relevant decision (false == no access == safe default, but might also be the case where the API shape changed).
suggested_fix: Add to queryKeys.ts: `myMembership: (projectId: string) => ['projects', projectId, 'membership'] as const`. Use it. Add `useMyMembership` query hook (the data shape is identical to what usePermission needs internally) so other features can reuse the membership record (e.g. the project members list, the role badge in the sidebar).

title: No usePagination / useInfiniteScroll hook for work packages, documents, or forum threads — pages implement pagination ad hoc
description: The pages list (`/home/cwlai/openproject-rewrite/pages/`) work-package table view, documents folder view, and forum threads view all have pagination. The hooks layer has `useInfiniteActivity` (useActivity.ts line 49, infinite) but no generic wrapper. Each consumer implements `page` state, `setPage` (from useUrlState), and a `useQuery({ queryKey: [..., page] })` themselves. This is repeated in useWorkPackages (line 142 — no pagination arg), useDocuments (no pagination), useForumMutations has `useCreateThread` invalidating `queryKeys.forumThreads` but no `useForumThreads(page)`. Missing: a typed `usePagination<T>(key, fetcher, { pageSize, initialPage })` that returns `{ items, page, setPage, totalPages, hasNext, hasPrev }`.
suggested_fix: Add `hooks/usePagination.ts` that wraps `useQuery` + page state + URL sync via the existing `useUrlState` hook. Add corresponding factories in queryKeys.ts: `workPackagesPaginated(filters, page)`, `documentsPaginated(projectId, folderId, page)`, `forumThreadsPaginated(forumId, page)`. Migrate the 3 page-level consumers to use them.

title: No bulk-operation hooks: useBulkUpdateWorkPackages, useBulkDeleteWorkPackages, useBulkArchive — required for table view 'select all' UI
description: The work packages table view (per pages/work-packages/index.tsx pattern) has multi-select checkboxes. Per-row actions exist (useUpdateWorkPackage, useDeleteWorkPackage), but the bulk equivalents (PATCH/DELETE on `/api/work-packages/bulk`) are missing. There's no `useBulkUpdate(ids, patch)` or `useBulkDelete(ids)` hook. Implementing bulk ops ad hoc in the page duplicates the invalidation logic, error handling, and CSRF/auth pattern. The backend likely already supports bulk endpoints (per the API review #03 finding pattern) — but the client has no hook.
suggested_fix: Add `hooks/useBulkWorkPackageActions.ts` with `useBulkUpdateWorkPackages`, `useBulkDeleteWorkPackages`, `useBulkChangeStatus`. Each should: (a) use api-client.js (HS-7), (b) onSuccess invalidate `['work-packages']` prefix, (c) onError toast (HS-11), (d) accept a Set<string> of ids. Add the same pattern for documents (`useBulkMoveDocuments`, `useBulkDeleteDocuments`).

title: No test coverage for 14 of 25 custom hooks — useSSE, useKeyboardShortcuts, useUpdateWorkPackage, useMyPage, useSearch, etc. have zero tests
description: `__tests__/hooks/use-hooks.test.tsx` (455 lines) and `__tests__/hooks/use-queries.test.tsx` (253 lines) exist, but cover only a subset. Hooks with NO test file (verified via `find . -name

`): useSSE.ts, useKeyboardShortcuts.ts, useUpdateWorkPackage (in useWorkPackages.ts), useMyPage.ts, useSearch.ts, useWatchWorkPackage.ts, useTimeEntries.ts, useTimeEntryMutations.ts, use-projects.ts, use-permission.ts, useNews.ts, useNewsMutations.ts, use-announcements.ts, use-current-user.ts, use-wip-limits.ts, use-current-user.ts, useRepositories.ts. ~15 of ~25 hooks have no test. The store actions (`stores/ui-store.ts` slices in `stores/ui/{toasts,modals,loading,sidebar}.ts`) are also untested — only `frontend/test/unit/store.test.js` covers the vanilla JS signal layer.
suggested_fix: Prioritize: (1) useSSE (reconnect logic has a confirmed bug, see HS-1), (2) useKeyboardShortcuts (sequence handler truncated to 2 keys, see HS-19), (3) useUpdateWorkPackage (optimistic update + rollback, see HS-17), (4) the four ui/* Zustand slices (add to __tests__/hooks or new __tests__/stores/). Add a CI check that fails when a new hook file is added under hooks/ without a corresponding test.

title: useInfiniteActivity has no maxPages safeguard, no IntersectionObserver for auto-fetch, no isFetchingNextPage exposed — incomplete implementation
description: Line 49-58: `useInfiniteQuery` is configured with `getNextPageParam` but the hook returns the raw `useInfiniteQuery` result. Callers must: (a) call `fetchNextPage()` manually, (b) implement their own IntersectionObserver or scroll listener, (c) handle the loading state. None of this is wrapped. There's no termination safeguard if the API misbehaves and returns `totalPages: Infinity` — the user could trigger thousands of fetches by scrolling. Also, the queryKey `['activity', 'infinite', projectId, filters]` (line 51) and the regular `['activity', projectId, filters]` (line 42) are TWO separate caches for what is essentially the same data — switching between `useActivity` and `useInfiniteActivity` for the same project causes two fetches.
suggested_fix: Either: (a) implement the scroll-listener wrapper in the hook (`const sentinelRef = useRef(); useIntersectionObserver(sentinelRef, fetchNextPage)`), or (b) document the manual integration. Add a `maxPages` config (default 50) and assert the API's `totalPages` doesn't exceed it. Unify the two queryKeys into one: `['activity', projectId, filters, mode: 'page' | 'infinite']` so a single fetch can serve both.

title: useMarkNotificationAsRead does not optimistically update the cache — UI shows unread badge for full request roundtrip
description: Line 5-17: `useMarkNotificationAsRead` does an `await fetch + invalidate` cycle. The notification stays in the unread list (and the unread-count badge stays incremented) for the full network roundtrip. The API likely returns the updated notification, which can be set via `queryClient.setQueryData` in onSuccess for instant UI feedback. Compare to useUpdateWorkPackage (line 200-213) which DOES use onMutate for optimistic update. The notifications UI is the most common place users notice UI lag.
suggested_fix: Add `onMutate` to mark the notification as read in the cache BEFORE the fetch returns. Invalidate `['notifications', 'unread-count']` on settle. Optionally use a per-notification setQueryData patch instead of a full invalidation. Consider WebSocket/SSE-driven unread count (the useSSE.ts already pushes `notification.new` events but not `notification.read` events).

title: useUIStore facade creates 13 separate Zustand subscriptions per render — defeats the slice-splitting perf benefit
description: Lines 22-49: the `useUIStore` facade calls 13 `use<X>Store` hooks (lines 37-48) to build the synthetic state object passed to the user's `selector`. Every render, all 13 subscriptions fire (Zustand only re-renders if the SELECTED slice changes, but the SUBSCRIPTIONS are created regardless). The whole point of splitting the store into toasts/modals/loading/sidebar slices (per file header line 1-4) was to allow components to subscribe to only what they need. By funneling everything through useUIStore, every component using useUIStore subscribes to ALL 13 values, getting the opposite of the intended benefit. The migration plan on lines 10-13 says 'new code should import directly from @/stores/ui' — this legacy shim is actively harming the new architecture.
suggested_fix: Accelerate the migration. Add a `// @deprecated` JSDoc to useUIStore pointing consumers to the new direct imports. Use `grep -r 'useUIStore' pages/ components/` to count remaining callers, file deprecation tickets. Remove the legacy shim once the count is 0. Add ESLint rule `no-restricted-imports` to prevent new useUIStore usage in pages/components/.

title: frontend/query.js (vanilla JS) and the TanStack Query layer in hooks/ are two parallel caching systems — split-brain state
description: The vanilla frontend/query.js (~280 LOC) implements a `queryClient` with `fetchQuery`, `useQuery`, `invalidate`, etc. — a hand-rolled mini TanStack Query. The hooks/ layer uses the REAL TanStack Query. Pages (per pages/ review) that mix these two systems (e.g. a page that uses the vanilla api-client for some calls and the TanStack hooks for others) get NO cross-invalidation: invalidating via `queryClient.invalidateQueries` does not touch the vanilla cache, and `frontend/queryClient.invalidate(key)` does not touch the TanStack cache. A mutation in one layer leaves the other layer's cache stale. Comment on frontend/query.js line 4 says 'Tiny query/cache layer that replaces TanStack Query' — implying a migration, but both are in active use.
suggested_fix: Decide on one. Given that hooks/* is the dominant pattern (TanStack Query is in active use across 25+ files, has React-aware features like Suspense, devtools, optimistic updates), deprecate frontend/query.js. Move any consumers of `queryClient.fetchQuery`/`useQuery` to the TanStack equivalents. Add a runtime `console.warn` in frontend/query.js when the file is imported, listing the top 3 call sites to migrate. Remove the file once 0 imports remain.

title: unwrap() throws `err.message = r.data?.error?.message || 'HTTP N'` but the withRoute HOF envelope structure is `{success, error}` not `{error: string}` — fragile fallback
description: Lines 217-222: `const err = new Error(r.data?.error?.message || \`HTTP ${r.status}\`)`. The withRoute HOF returns `{success: false, error: {code, message, details?}}` (per useMeetingMutations.ts line 56-58, which knows the shape). But api-client.js reads `r.data.error.message` — this only works if r.data is unwrapped from the envelope already. Compare line 119 in useMeetingMutations.ts: it manually unwraps `body.error` because the API returns the envelope. The api-client.js's unwrap() may end up with `err.message = '[object Object]'` if the API actually returns `{success: false, error: {message: '...'}}` and r.data is the full envelope — then `r.data.error.message` is correct, but the original withRoute success field is ignored. If the API returns the envelope nested as `{data: {success: false, error: ...}}` (common Next.js API route pattern), then `r.data.error` is undefined and the fallback 'HTTP N' is shown. Confirmed by useWikiMutations.ts line 48-49 which does `const json = await res.json(); return json.data` — so the wiki API wraps the data in a `data` field.
suggested_fix: Make unwrap() handle both shapes: check `r.data?.error?.message`, then `r.data?.data?.error?.message`, then fall back to `HTTP ${r.status}`. Better: standardize ALL API routes to use the same envelope, and document the contract in a comment in api-client.js. Add a test that POSTs to a real withRoute endpoint and asserts the error message is propagated.
